perturbation.original_text: Upon the Company's request, we shall promptly deliver to the Company or destroy all written Evaluation Materials and any other written materials without retaining, in whole or in part, any copies, extracts or other reproductions (whatever the form or storage medium) of such materials.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the Company's request, we shall deliver to the Company or destroy all written Evaluation Materials and any other written materials without retaining, in whole or in part, any copies, extracts or other reproductions (whatever the form or storage medium) of such materials; destruction shall occur within 90 days.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the destruction of confidential materials to 90 days may conflict with data protection laws that mandate immediate destruction of sensitive data upon request.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Personal Information Protection Act
perturbation.law_explanation: N.Y. Gen. Bus. Law § 899-aa requires businesses to implement reasonable security measures to protect private information. The modified clause introduces a 90-day delay in destruction, which may not be considered 'reasonable' under the law, especially if the company requests immediate destruction. This delay increases the risk of unauthorized access or disclosure during the interim period.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: New York Consolidated Laws, General Business Law - GBS § 899-aa | FindLaw New York Consolidated Laws, General Business Law - GBS § 899-aa. Notification; person without valid authorization has acquired private information Current as of January 01, 2024 | Updated by FindLaw Staff 1. As used in this section, the following terms shall have the following meanings: (a) “ Personal information ” shall mean any information concerning a natural person which, because of name, number, personal mark, or other identifier, can be used to identify such natural person; (b) “ Private information ” shall mean either: (i) personal information consisting of any information in combination with any one or more of the following data elements, when either the data element or the combination of personal information plus the data element is not encrypted, or is encrypted with an encryption key that has also been accessed or acquired: (1) social security number; (2) driver's license number or non-driver identification card number; (3) account number, credit or debit card number, in combination with any required security code, access code, password or other information that would permit access to an individual's financial account; (4) account number, credit or debit card number, if circumstances exist wherein such number could be used to access an individual's financial account without additional identifying information, security code, access code, or password; or (5) biometric information, meaning data generated by electronic measurements of an individual's unique physical characteristics, such as a fingerprint, voice print, retina or iris image, or other unique physical representation or digital representation of biometric data which are used to authenticate or ascertain the individual's identity; or (ii) a user name or e-mail address in combination with a password or security question and answer that would permit access to an online account. “Private information” does not include publicly available information which is lawfully made available to the
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 90-day delay in the destruction of confidential materials, which contradicts the principle of 'reasonable security measures' for private information as outlined in N.Y. Gen. Bus. Law § 899-aa. While the scraped snippets do not explicitly define 'reasonable', the Law Explanation argues that delaying destruction increases the risk of unauthorized access. The original text mandated prompt destruction, which aligns more closely with immediate data protection needs.

perturbation.original_text: We understand and agree that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by us or our representatives and that the Company will be entitled to specific performance and injunctive relief as remedies for any such breach. Such remedies shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach of this Agreement by us or our representatives but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or equity.
perturbation.changed_text: We understand and agree that money damages would be the exclusive remedy for any breach of this Agreement by us or our representatives, limiting the Company to monetary compensation only.
perturbation.explanation: Restricting remedies to only money damages and excluding specific performance or injunctive relief may be unenforceable, particularly in cases of breach of confidentiality, as it severely limits the Company's recourse. Specific performance is often necessary.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General principles of contract law regarding remedies
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 359 states that specific performance will not be decreed if damages would be adequate to protect the injured party. However, in many cases, especially involving confidential information, money damages are not adequate, and specific performance or injunctive relief is required. The modification attempts to waive remedies that a court would normally grant to make the injured party whole.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: specific performance | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you specific performance Specific performance is a contractual remedy in which a court orders a party to fulfill their obligations as closely as possible to what was promised in the contract , rather than simply paying damages for failing to do so. This remedy is often applied when monetary damages are inadequate to fix the harm caused by the breach ; most commonly in cases involving real property and rare chattels . Specific performance ensures that the aggrieved party receives exactly what was agreed upon in the contract, often in situations where the subject matter is unique or irreplaceable. [Last reviewed in June of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities THE LEGAL PROCESS courts business law contracts wex definitions business sectors civil procedure commercial transactions courts and procedure property law Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the original agreement and general legal principles regarding remedies. The original text allows for specific performance and injunctive relief, while the changed text limits the company to monetary compensation only. The 'Wex' snippet describes specific performance as a remedy where a court orders a party to fulfill their obligations, especially when monetary damages are inadequate. By restricting the remedy to only money damages, the modified contract removes a standard legal recourse and thus creates a contradiction. The Perturbation explanation notes this change may make the agreement unenforceable as monetary damages are not always adequate.

perturbation.original_text: Accordingly, unless required by applicable law, we agree that prior to the execution of a Sale Agreement with respect to the closing of a Possible Transaction, without the prior written consent of the Company, we will not, and we will direct our representatives not to, disclose to any person either the fact that discussions or negotiations are taking place concerning a possible transaction between us and the Company or any to the terms, conditions or other facts with respect to any such Possible Transaction, including the status thereof unless we are advised by counsel that we are required to make such disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Accordingly, even if required by applicable law, we agree that prior to the execution of a Sale Agreement with respect to the closing of a Possible Transaction, without the prior written consent of the Company, we will not, and we will direct our representatives not to, disclose to any person either the fact that discussions or negotiations are taking place concerning a possible transaction between us and the Company or any to the terms, conditions or other facts with respect to any such Possible Transaction, including the status thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the "unless required by applicable law" clause forces the parties to potentially violate securities laws that mandate disclosure of material information.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 10b-5 prohibits making materially false or misleading statements or omissions in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. If the existence of a possible transaction is material information, and failure to disclose it would be misleading, this clause would force Carpenter Technology to violate securities laws.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the clause "unless required by applicable law," creating a direct conflict with securities laws such as Rule 10b-5, which mandates disclosure of material information. The original text acknowledges this legal obligation, but the modified text ignores it. Scraped Law Snippet 2 explicitly states that it is unlawful to "omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading." By removing the exception for legal requirements, the contract now compels nondisclosure even when legally mandated, directly contradicting Rule 10b-5.

perturbation.original_text: No Solicitation During the period from acceptance of this agreement until the earlier of (a) the execution of a definitive agreement or (b) the date that is 45 days after the date of such acceptance, the Company and the officers, directors, employees and other representatives of the Company including JPM shall not directly or indirectly, solicit, initiate, or encourage any offers or proposals for the acquisition of the Company, or of any of the capital stock or all, or substantially all, of the assets of the Company, from third parties (an "Acquisition Proposal").
perturbation.changed_text: No Solicitation During the period from acceptance of this agreement until the earlier of (a) the execution of a definitive agreement or (b) the date that is 15 days after the date of such acceptance, the Company and the officers, directors, employees and other representatives of the Company including JPM shall not directly or indirectly, solicit, initiate, or encourage any offers or proposals for the acquisition of the Company, or of any of the capital stock or all, or substantially all, of the assets of the Company, from third parties (an "Acquisition Proposal").
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the 'no solicitation' period to 15 days could be considered an unreasonable restraint of trade, especially in complex transactions where a longer period is required to fully explore potential offers. This could be problematic under antitrust laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 1 of the Sherman Act prohibits contracts, combinations, and conspiracies in restraint of trade. While a no-solicitation clause is generally permissible, the duration and scope must be reasonable. A court may find that a 15-day period is unreasonably short, hindering the target company's ability to obtain the best possible deal and thus restraining trade.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 15 / CHAPTER 1 / § 1 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 15 USC 1 : Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty Text contains those laws in effect on July 12, 2025 From Title 15-COMMERCE AND TRADE CHAPTER 1-MONOPOLIES AND COMBINATIONS IN RESTRAINT OF TRADE Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous Amendments Effective Date Short Title Executive Documents §1. Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal. Every person who shall make any contract or engage in any combination or conspiracy hereby declared to be illegal shall be
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the 'no solicitation' period from 45 to 15 days. The Law Explanation states that this shortened period "hindering the target company's ability to obtain the best possible deal and thus restraining trade." This contradicts the Sherman Act, as described in Scraped Law Snippet 1: "Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal." The modified contract, by potentially restraining trade through an unreasonably short no-solicitation period, creates a legal contradiction.
